name: Bi Gan
description: Bi Gan, also known as Prince Gan, was a prominent figure in Chinese mythology and history, revered as a symbol of loyalty, integrity, and righteousness. He served as an advisor to the tyrannical King Zhou of the Shang Dynasty. Bi Gan was known for his unwavering honesty and courage, often criticizing the king's corrupt and decadent behavior, even at great personal risk. Legend states that Bi Gan possessed an extraordinary heart, often described as having seven apertures, which allowed him to perceive truth and justice with unparalleled clarity. His unwavering commitment to ethical governance eventually led to his tragic demise. King Zhou, influenced by his consort Daji, who was actually a fox spirit in disguise, became increasingly enraged by Bi Gan's constant admonishments. Daji convinced the king that Bi Gan's heart, with its unique properties, could be used to cure her supposed illness. The king, blinded by his infatuation and cruelty, ordered Bi Gan to be executed and his heart extracted. According to some versions of the myth, Bi Gan's heart was taken, yet he miraculously survived and fled the capital, becoming the ancestor of the Lin family. He is often depicted as a scholar or official, sometimes holding his heart in his hands, a poignant reminder of his sacrifice. Bi Gan is venerated as a patron saint of merchants, particularly those who value honesty and fair dealing. His story serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of moral courage and the consequences of tyranny and corruption. His legacy continues to inspire those who stand up for what is right, even in the face of adversity.
subtitle: The Loyal and Righteous Advisor

keyMyths.title: Bi Gan's Advice to King Zhou
keyMyths.description: Bi Gan consistently advised King Zhou against his tyrannical and corrupt practices, earning the king's ire. He pleaded for the king to prioritize the well-being of his people over personal indulgence.

keyMyths.title: The Extraction of Bi Gan's Heart
keyMyths.description: Daji, a fox spirit in disguise, convinced King Zhou that Bi Gan's heart could cure her illness. The king ordered Bi Gan's execution and the extraction of his heart, a symbol of his cruelty.

keyMyths.title: Bi Gan's Flight and Legacy
keyMyths.description: Despite having his heart removed, Bi Gan miraculously survived and fled the capital. He became the ancestor of the Lin family and a symbol of unwavering integrity.

sources.author: Xu Zhonglin
sources.description: A major source for the myths surrounding Bi Gan and the fall of the Shang Dynasty.
sources.text: A major source for the myths surrounding Bi Gan and the fall of the Shang Dynasty.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Record the lineage tracing back to Bi Gan and his significance to the Lin surname.
sources.text: Record the lineage tracing back to Bi Gan and his significance to the Lin surname.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestral Veneration
worshipPractices.description: Bi Gan is often venerated as an ancestor, with families maintaining altars and offering prayers to honor his memory and seek his blessings for integrity and prosperity.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Bi Gan often receive offerings of food, incense, and symbolic items representing wealth and good fortune, reflecting his association with prosperity and justice.

festivals.name: Bi Gan's Birthday
festivals.timing: Observed on various dates depending on local traditions, often in the lunar calendar.
festivals.description: Celebrations include temple fairs, theatrical performances depicting his life and sacrifice, and special rituals to honor his virtues and seek his blessings.

festivals.name: Ghost Festival (Zhongyuan Jie)
festivals.timing: 15th day of the seventh lunar month
festivals.description: During the Ghost Festival, Bi Gan is sometimes invoked as a righteous figure who can protect against malevolent spirits and ensure justice in the afterlife.

cultural.modernLegacy: Bi Gan remains a potent symbol of integrity and ethical governance in modern China and among Chinese diaspora communities. His story is frequently referenced in political discourse and business ethics training, serving as a cautionary tale against corruption and a model for principled leadership. Statues and images of Bi Gan can be found in businesses and government offices, reminding individuals to uphold honesty and fairness in their dealings.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Bi Gan are rooted in the late Shang Dynasty, a period marked by increasing corruption and tyranny under the rule of King Zhou. Bi Gan was a prince, a member of the Shang royal family, and a direct descendant of Emperor Yi. His birth was considered auspicious, and he was raised with the expectation of serving the kingdom with wisdom and virtue. From a young age, Bi Gan demonstrated exceptional intelligence and a strong moral compass. He excelled in his studies of governance, ethics, and strategy, quickly becoming known for his unwavering commitment to justice and the well-being of the people. His early life was characterized by a deep sense of responsibility and a growing concern for the state of the Shang Dynasty under King Zhou's increasingly erratic and cruel reign. This sense of duty would ultimately lead to his tragic but heroic fate.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most famous story associated with Bi Gan revolves around his unwavering opposition to King Zhou's tyranny. As the king indulged in extravagance and cruelty, influenced by his consort Daji, Bi Gan repeatedly advised him to change his ways. He pointed out the suffering of the people and the dangers of neglecting the affairs of the state. However, King Zhou, blinded by his own desires and influenced by Daji's manipulations, ignored Bi Gan's warnings. Daji, a fox spirit in disguise, resented Bi Gan's righteousness and plotted his demise. She convinced King Zhou that Bi Gan possessed an extraordinary heart with seven apertures, believing it would grant immortality. Driven by this false promise, King Zhou ordered Bi Gan's heart to be cut out to examine its supposed magical properties. Bi Gan, knowing his fate, remained steadfast in his principles and willingly sacrificed himself to demonstrate the importance of integrity and loyalty. Legend says that after his heart was removed, he rode on a white horse and was told not to answer anyone. However, upon hearing a woman ask about the price of heartless vegetables, he questioned how vegetables could grow without a heart, thus breaking the spell and dying. This act cemented his status as a symbol of unwavering loyalty and sacrifice for the greater good.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Bi Gan is widely worshipped throughout China and among Chinese communities worldwide, primarily as a deity associated with wealth, integrity, and justice. Temples dedicated to Bi Gan are often found in commercial areas and government buildings, reflecting his role as a patron of ethical business practices and honest governance. Devotees offer prayers and make offerings to Bi Gan, seeking his blessings for financial prosperity, protection from injustice, and guidance in making righteous decisions. He is often depicted in statues and paintings as a dignified figure with a stern expression, symbolizing his unwavering commitment to truth and justice. In some traditions, Bi Gan is also considered a protector of pregnant women and children, believed to ward off evil spirits and ensure safe childbirth. His image is sometimes placed in homes to bring good fortune and ward off negative influences. The continued veneration of Bi Gan underscores the enduring importance of integrity and ethical conduct in Chinese culture.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Bi Gan's legacy extends far beyond his tragic death, solidifying his status as a cultural icon and a symbol of unwavering integrity. He is revered as one of the Three Wise Men of the Shang Dynasty, alongside Qi Zi and Wei Zi, all of whom stood against King Zhou's tyranny. His story serves as a powerful moral lesson, emphasizing the importance of standing up for what is right, even in the face of adversity and personal risk. Bi Gan's influence can be seen in various aspects of Chinese society, from business ethics to political discourse. His example is often cited in discussions about the importance of honesty, transparency, and accountability in leadership. Many businesses display images or statues of Bi Gan as a reminder to conduct their affairs with integrity and fairness. His story continues to be retold in operas, plays, and literature, ensuring that his legacy of courage and righteousness endures for generations to come. He remains a potent symbol of the enduring power of moral conviction.

shortDescription: Bi Gan, a deity in Chinese mythology, is revered as a symbol of loyalty and integrity. He famously advised King Zhou of the Shang Dynasty.
